definition: *Geomancy* is divination by earth: the diviner generates a field of dots or points, at first by jabbing the ground at random, then reads the resulting figures through a fixed set of houses and meanings. Among the *-mancy* arts it is the most rule-bound and diagrammatic — a technical procedure closer to astrology's tables than to the entrail-gazing of the *haruspex*.
history: The name joins Greek *gē* ('earth') to *manteia* ('divination'), but the developed art the Middle Ages knew was not Greek in origin. It entered Latin Europe from the Arabic *'ilm al-raml*, the 'science of sand,' and MedievalMagicDB records it as one of the most widely copied and debated of the specific divinatory arts — technical enough to circulate as a manual of figures and judgments, yet suspect enough to draw repeated condemnation. Skinner notes that of Isidore of Seville's tidy four elemental forms of divination, geomancy was the one that genuinely fit. The word survives in Magic as the *geomancer* and in the game's earthbound imagery.

etymology.note: Divination by dots and earth-figures; it entered Europe from the Arabic 'ilm al-raml, the 'science of sand'.
etymology.hook: Dots in the sand that grew into a whole art.

flavor_snippets.html: Telepathy changes minds, necromancy changes lives, but <mark>geomancy</mark> changes the world.

"The common folk call it the breaking of the crust or the spilling of fire. <mark>Geomancers</mark> call it target practice."
—Ryda, <mark>geomancer</mark>

"It is not in me to tread lightly."
—Ryda, <mark>geomancer</mark>
